titleStmt.title: Letter from Fr Francis M. Shaw to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 3 April 1918
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis Shaw

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Francis M. Shaw to Father Provincial regarding the masses Francis M. Shaw had. Francis, or Frank, Shaw (1881-1924) SJ was born in Ennis, county Clare. He was ordained on 31 July 1916 at Milltown Park, Dublin. Fr Shaw joined the war early in 1916, working in the 16th General Field Hospital at Le Tréporte. In 1917, possibly due to his nationalistic views, Fr Shaw was dispatched to India, and later to Mesopotamia.Fr. Thomas V. Nolan (1867-1941) was the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus (1912-1922) and was a member of the Distribution Committee which looked after the welfare and distribution of the Belgian refugees who arrived in Ireland as a consequence of the First World War.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
3 April. 1918 Dear Reverend Father Provincial, P.C. There is nothing fresh to report; but the
weekly mails are much an improvement on the uncertain mails
ofthe sea-faring life, that I like to take advantage of them. I hope you got my last week's letter, with enclosure for Fr Farley.
I was able to say 18 Masses for him in March. I shall probably
have the full number for April. This morning I rose with the
lark (we have larks in Mesopotamia â the real kind, not
Joe Gargery's 'Wot larks !') and trekked a couple of miles to a
siege battery just arrived in our district, and had Mass and
Holy Communion for the ten Catholics in the unit. The gunners
here as in France seem tobe neglected in the Chaplain line.
I shall do a like trek for another battery tomorrow or the
day after : it has about twenty Catholics. Fr Frank Browne and FrFitzgibbon will I am sure be
in the thick ofthe present German push. We are lucky
people in one sense who are in the wilderness. Some of
the people out here left Italy just before the banks
gave way there, and have practically no experience
ofwar. I am comforted every day by the appearance of
fresh familiar wild-flowers â dandelions, thistles, buttercups,
etc etc. Sometimes I can almost imagine Iam at
home. I commend myself to your Reverence's prayers :
Yours very sincerely in Christ; Francis M Shaw S.J.
